speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go out of the hiding place then at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When the person came to-- and called us by name. And we came out, and it was truly safe for the time being. And that's when-- then we had to move out of this area and move into another area. We left everything and took what we could carry. And moved into this-- in the attic, another attic in-- in another building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone left in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very many people were left, but the ghetto was so-- so much smaller. It was shrunk so much that we were still crowded. And soon after that the ghetto was completely liquidated and everyone was shipped to Plaszow, the camp that was being built next to the-- in the vicinity of Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking, by marching.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was just a couple of kilometers actually right out of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in that march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you how this was done. They-- they would only allow those people to leave who had jobs, who worked. So companies, like the brush makers company and the shoemakers company and the uniform makers, all lined up in different-- different sections. And they were all allowed to or transported or-- or escorted out together, you know, in one group. So I lined up, of course, with my brush makers company. My mother lined up with the cleaning ladies. And the cleaning ladies were marched out. And I was watching my mother leave. And then soon after that the brush makers were beginning to move up. And as I-- as I got close to the gate, there was a guard standing there, guards on both sides. And one of them just pulled me out and said, that's OK. You come later. You can't come right now. Come later. And so I was out of the group and my friends were still kind of running around trying to figure out what to do. So they decided they would go hide again like we did the last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you? What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't-- I didn't hide. I decided I would not hide, that I would try to get out. So I-- I-- I joined another group, just got in another group and tried to get out. And I was pulled out a couple more times and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you kept getting pulled out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was little boy. And children were not being sent to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. And too little, you know. I had a-- I had one of those backpacks on my back, and I was hunched over a little bit and a skinny little guy. So they just pulled me out. But eventually, as you can see, I-- I joined another group. And I finally made it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did not want to stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to stay in-- in the ghetto. And then the-- my friends went and hid. But it was a good thing that I did, because those that remained, the older people and the-- and those who went on hiding and children were all murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who murdered them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in and cleared out the ghetto and they-- whomever they found, they murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because some of them-- some of the ones that they found, they brought to Plaszow and shot them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but that's-- no, I didn't see this personally. There was a special place where they took people to be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were told about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. No one survived. None-- those friends that I had, I never saw them again. And so the ghetto was closed, and as I said everyone found was liquidated and murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to-- to a barrack in Plaszow It was the fortunate thing that I did, right. I was not smart. I just, I think I probably just wanted to go with my-- where my mother went. I just saw my mother leave, so I-- I just tried to get out and I made it. But it was-- the thing that was ironic is that I-- I left a ghetto which was hell, and I got to-- to this Plaszow, which was more-- worse than hell because that was the camp where Amon Goth was the commandant, the person who liked to shoot people from the balcony and also point blank and-- and did other atrocities during his tenure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you actually arrive in Plaszow? What year that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1943. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- in the fall of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a labor camp. Yeah. And so at first the-- the-- first of all the camp was still in transition. It was still be built and construction was still going on. So most of those people who were not assigned to specific jobs in specific factories were assigned to work at random to-- at different locations in construction.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I worked at places like building roads and helping construct barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone know that you're only 13 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was hoping no one would notice. And so I did the work of a-- of a grown man, hauling dirt, and breaking up grave markers, and building that road that was actually depicted in the movie called

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel to know that you were breaking someone's tombstone? Did it-- did it affect you in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not really. It seemed like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It seemed like-- as if, you know, things were so unreal that to break up somebody's tombstone wasn't anything in comparison to what was being-- what was happening to live people. We were always hear about so many people got shot today. It was-- it's like listening to a-- to a score in a soccer game or a football game, you know. You want to find out what that score was. And when we used to-- some of the-- sometimes these details, these work details, were sent outside of the camp. And on the way back through this gate, we would then try to find out what the score was. And the score meaning how many people died or how many people were killed by this madman or his friends. You know, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Amon Goth was not the only one perpetrating all these atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: All these officers, all the SS who were in-- under his command were doing exactly the same thing. They were all doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a barrack older-- [GERMAN] they are called, you know. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was usually-- no, it was usually an inmate, someone from a-- a prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head of your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he understand that you were a child still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. That-- that had no-- no effect on anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he show compassion in any way to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. I don't even remember being in contact with him. The thing was that you kept to yourself and you tried to make sure your-- your spot on that-- on that barrack-- bunk was still available. Because it wasn't a bed or anything. It was just a plank. And people just lay one next to the other. And you have to kind of move around a little bit to locate yourself an area to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone look out for you? Did anyone help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. Did you ever get the chance to see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I-- I don't know how I even found the barracks. But I did find the barracks where she was. And I went in and being a little guy, they didn't notice me much. And I actually saw her there. The first thing she wanted to do was she had some piece of bread that she was hiding under the-- under a blanket and gave me a little piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she tell you when she saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was-- she was so happy to see me because the-- by that time, everyone knew that people who were left in the ghetto were-- were executed, died. That-- she was amazed that I had actually made it out. And I was amazed too because I really didn't know what I was doing. In retrospect, it turned out to be the right thing, but at the time, I was simply just doing it by instinct. And eventually, as I was working at these different jobs building roads and things like that, the contact with Goth-- my first contact with Goth was that he came by where we were working. And apparently he didn't like the way things were going. So-- probably too slow for his liking. And so he lined everybody up on the-- on the assembly area. And ordered whippings, ordered us to be whipped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you whipped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was whipped. I received 25 lashes with one of those whips that had these little lead weights on the end made out of leather. Cut like a knife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your crime?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was part of the-- the workforce and building these roads and he didn't like the way things were going. Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you? How did you survive such a beating?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to know. You know, I-- I've often thought about this. And I was wondering, you know thinking. When I think about these things, I think about the little boy who was 13 as if he were someone else. And as I tell it, it-- it turns out to be myself. But it-- I think of it, and I think these guards who were ordered to whip, did they really whip me as hard as they did an adult or not? You know, I wonder about that. But I remember that first-- first feel of the whip was so strong that it was like being cut with a knife and my-- my reflex, the natural reflex was-- you know we were supposed to hold on to the table, there were these long tables. And there were people being whipped on each side of these long table and to hold onto it. And our-- our clothes were off. You know, our pants were down. And so the first feel of the whip, my hands just flew right back naturally. And so the second one I got across my knuckles. And after that, it didn't make much difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you an awkward question. But-- did you ever whip your children when you disciplined them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you? Did you do it intentionally? Did you intentionally not whip them? It's not uncommon for a child--

words.text: It's not uncommon for a child--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you know. I-- I-- I would give Daniel a pat on the butt, you know, to make him behave or do something that he wasn't-- you know, to stop doing something he was doing. But I always felt funny about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never took a strap?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was my first encounter with our-- our commandant. And I had encountered him again several times. And finally, after I finished with this construction, the factory, the brush factor, was actually restarted in-- in Plaszow camp and I was able to get a job there, back to where I-- the same job I had when I was in the-- in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the beginning of 19-- at the end of 1943-- the end of 1943 or maybe the beginning '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Schindler had-- was able to establish a sub camp for his workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was still working for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was working at Schindler's company and my brother was with Schindler's company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they living there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first they were living in Plaszow and going back and forth. But then when this sub camp was created, my brother and my father went to live in that sub camp in Schindler's company. And my mother and I were in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that sub company that he made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emalia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. And my sister, as you know, was still at this cable company or the electrical appliance company.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I got my brush making job back. And my mother was still working as a cleaning lady. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother went from Plaszow into Krakow to do cleaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were doing cleaning in the-- inside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, inside the camp. OK. Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Schindler as-- after he built his sub camp decided to increase his workforce and was taking on more people. So my father asked him to put my mother and me on the-- on the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this list?

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, there were several Schindler's lists, you know, not-- not just one. For example, when my brother was taken-- was added to the Schindler's company, that was another list because my father asked him to take my brother on. And then when the next group was going to be sent to Schindler's company, then a list was created of those people who were going to be sent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what number you were on his list?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- on the-- on Schindler's company I was-- I only had a number when I went on the last-- on the last leg of our trip, that is the one where we moved the company from Krakow to Plaszow because at that point, we went through a-- Gross-Rosen camp. And they-- there we got an actual number. Before that, all we had was a letter. We had a letter tattooed on our arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-- K-- K-- KL, I guess it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you got that on your arm now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to. I used to have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just, it faded out. Because when I first got it, I-- I sucked it out with my mouth and washed it off. And it-- it remained on my arm all through the war, but it was very, very faint.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever say maybe we should do it again? They couldn't see it properly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody checked it. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did KL stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Konzentrationslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who received that tattoo? All the-- all the inmates in-- in Schindler's company. And who gave you that tattoo?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Jewish doctors that tattooed us. In any case--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it painful?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it being much-- very painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- when I was-- so Schindler agreed to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To take you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --put my mother and me on a list and I eventually made it. But it-- that-- that didn't go very smoothly either, because I'd already worked-- been working in this brush factory and when the Schindler company was being formed, by that time, it was obvious that it was-- it would be preferable to be in the sub camp at Schindler's company than to be in Plaszow with-- with Goth as a commandant. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow someone decided that I could not be allowed out. That I'd already-- I already had-- the pretense was that I already had a job, and I couldn't be going to Schindler's company. But my mother was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to see her off too. Just to-- again, same as before. And they were forming this group, and she was going to leave. And I almost decided that this is going-- I was going to stay in Plaszow. But somehow, I'm not certain, but I approached the commandant or the officer who was going to escort those people to the-- to Emalia, and I reported. I told him that I was on the list and I am not allowed to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my argument to this-- to this Nazi officer who would shoot a Jew at-- for nothing, I was explaining to him that I was not allowed out, and that my mother is in a group and she is leaving and my brother and my father are already there. That was my argument. Pretty naive little boy of 13 talking to this nasty looking German officer. And I could just about-- my eye-- my eye level was just about even with his belt. And he looked at his assistant and asked him if that was true. And the assistant said yes. He saw my name on the list. So he ordered me into this group, and I made it out. But while I was doing-- while I was in the group waiting to leave, I looked over and there was the assistant trying to talk to this officer about something about this incident. And he was trying to explain to him why I couldn't go. But he refused to listen. He even got mad at his assistant for-- you know, for arguing with him. In any case, I'd like to think that it would be nice to know that maybe for an instant this-- this Nazi, foggy, brained Nazi German who accepted Nazi philosophy that some people are less than human and they can be shot at will probably saw a little boy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had pity?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and decided that this was the natural thing for a little boy to do is to go where his parents are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel like a little boy at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. I-- I never accepted that. You know, they had a place at the very beginning of this camp. They had barracks just for-- for children about my age. But I wouldn't go near those places. I wouldn't go near those barracks. And I always-- the whole time that I was in this situation, I tried to associate myself with adults. Again, it was just instinct. It was not just being that smart. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sir--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew-- plus I knew that I could figure out that they don't value adults, able-bodied adults, why would they worry about children? And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: You understood that already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that even then.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And as it turned out, they took all those children out and murdered them. And-- there was one incident where those children were all gathered up, all the barracks and taken away in trucks and never seen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there at that time? Did you see those children taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was-- by that time I was already in Schindler's company.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were told that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did-- where did you actually end up then? You joined your mother, your father. Where were you taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was taken to Krakow where the-- where the Schindler's company was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emalia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Emalia. And I worked for Schindler until the company had to be dismantled because the Russians were invading in back. And the front where the Germans was going very badly. And the Russians were going to be in Krakow soon. So companies like Schindler's company were just dismantle their company and let their-- their prisoners go back to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you worked hard in that company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we did. I-- I used to work night shift mostly. 12 hour shifts, standing near a machine and doing labor work. And I encountered Schindler many times as he was walking through the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, he did. Yeah. There was a-- there was a-- somehow-- some kind of a special relationship between Schindler and my father. As you know, Schindler was not an angel in dealing with black-marketeering. He was a womanizer. And he didn't mind taking bribes and giving bribes. And he was not your squeaky clean kind of character. And but-- but with my father, my father having lost all his savings at the very beginning of the war, all he could offer Schindler was his skills. And Schindler accepted that. And I think that somehow he respected this-- this relationship so that each time my father asked him for something, he would do it. He wasn't doing it because he-- my father had something else to offer him. So when he asked for my mother and me to be taken on, he only listened. And he didn't say anything. And he had a terrific memory. He just remembered. And we know that it was his doing that put my mother and me on the list. And then eventually, my sister, Aviva, who was returned from her company at about same time to Plaszow. And she would have been just in the general population and would have had about a 100 to one chance to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you all-- how many of you then, family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were five family members, five Leysons who survived the war in Schindler's company. At the end of the war, there were five of us there, my parents, my sister, my brother, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother, which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do who know who-- I want to ask you a question. Who wrote that list? How did that list come to be? Well, I don't want to go all the way back there, but do you have any clue who actually-- if Schindler gave the order, who wrote it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who actually typed it, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, those people who did the-- the accounting for him and the-- there was some Jewish accountants that did some business work for him, bookkeepers and so on, those were the people who did the list.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Schindler good to the children? Did he in any way--

speaker: interviewee
text: There weren't many children in Schindler's company. I was-- for-- at one time, there was a person younger than I in-- in Schindler's company. And then I was the youngest. He was taken away, and I was the youngest in the company. I was the youngest one working in the factory along with the adults.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the adults in any way sympathetic towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah, I had-- I had good-- people didn't treat me badly and-- as a-- as a child--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- give an example of what daily life was like for-- for the community within-- Did you all live in the same place? You all lived in the building of Emalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In special barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only among men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were-- there were barracks for men and barracks for women in the same camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have opportunities each day to see all of your family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mm-hmm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were holiday celebrated during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, as-- as strange as it sounds, there-- they were. There were people who practiced their religion, who celebrated the holidays. As-- as hard as it was and as dangerous as it was, it was done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me-- one thing first, didn't the German guards see what was going on? Didn't they interfere in any way with this religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, occasionally they would. If they-- if they saw it, it was a very risky thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't something you did outright.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not. It was all done in--

words.text: absolutely not. It was all done in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an example-- give me an example of a holiday or a religious observance that-- that was kept. Something that had meaning to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah was-- was something I can remember as being kept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: In some-- in some-- in some quarters, in some situations, the sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people who were-- who were older than I who had practiced their religion before the war had gotten-- got together and had prayer meetings and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you and your family? Did you participate in those prayer meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my father would, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it risky to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. It was always risky to do anything spiritual, whether it's singing songs or writing poems or-- or telling jokes. You know? And we did all of those things. And I think that's part of resisting. You know, resisting isn't always with mechanical weapons. The facts are really that the Germans were conducting a war against the Jews. And they had a special army to do this. A special army that was equipped and had special marching orders. And they-- their job was to kill Jews, to murder. And we resisted and fought back with all the-- the equipment we had. And we had men, women, and children. So naturally, the-- the odds were against us, and we took a lot of casualties, of course. But in the end, they didn't win the war that they wanted to win. They actually lost that, the spiritual, the-- the people's spirit. As hard as it was in-- in ghetto, I used to be able to-- there was a little yard and they'd put some chairs up there and tables. And they had a cabaret, a stage production. You know, people were doing stage work. And I used to chin myself up on the back of the wall to see what they were doing. And there'd be a stand up comedian telling jokes, sad jokes, you know, kind of gallows type, but nevertheless, it was-- it was going on all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape four.
